lesson.menu_desc: Deliver bad news with an empathetic structure and steady the room: lead in with a softening opener, state the setback in active voice, take ownership without over-apologizing, then reassure with a concrete forward plan — using will for firm promises, going to / be + -ing for arranged steps, and the first conditional for contingencies — 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 14 — Damage Control — Delivering Bad News & Reassuring Stakeholders
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Victor Malvezi Paschotto | Aula 14 — Damage Control

listenings.text: Okay, the numbers are in and they are not good, so I have to call Beatriz before the board does. There is no easy way to say this, and pretending otherwise only makes it worse. My plan is simple. First, I lead in honestly: I want to be straight with you, the quarter came in below plan. No burying it on slide nine. Second, I own it in active voice. We missed the target. Not mistakes were made, not the market moved against us. We missed it, and I take responsibility for the call that got us here. Over-apologizing helps no one, so one clear sorry, then forward. Third, I reassure with a real plan, not vague comfort. I name the root cause, I say what we are going to do this week, and I make a firm promise with will: we will close the shortfall by quarter end. And I add a contingency: if the pilot slips again, we will pull the stopgap budget and protect the core accounts. Honest about the past, concrete about the future. That is how you keep trust when the news is bad.

Hi Victor, it is Mariana. A quick note before you deliver this. Bad news is not the enemy; a clumsy delivery is. Three things to hold onto. First, get ahead of it. Do not let stakeholders hear the setback from someone else. A short, honest lead-in earns you the room: there is no easy way to say this, or, I want to be straight with you. Second, own it cleanly and move on. Say we missed the target, in active voice, and resist the passive blame-shifting that everyone sees through. Take responsibility, apologize once, and do not grovel; over-apologizing makes you look shaky, not sincere. Third, and this is the one people skip, reassure with a concrete plan, not feelings. Name the root cause, commit with will to what you will fix, lay out what you are going to do this week, and add a contingency with if: if the supplier slips, we will switch to the backup. People can forgive a bad quarter. What they cannot forgive is feeling misled or left without a plan. Be transparent, be accountable, and give them the next step. You have got this.
